season: irruptive (stochastic -- present or absent based on seed crop conditions, not calendar)

coordinates.description: Seattle reference; actual presence determined by continental-scale seed crop conditions, not geographic fidelity to a single location

plumage.overall: Heavily streaked brown-and-beige with subtle yellow at base of flight feathers and tail, visible in flight
plumage.streaking: Heavy, dense brown streaks on whitish-buff underparts and back
plumage.yellow: Variable -- subtle yellow flash at base of primaries and outer tail feathers, visible in flight, invisible at rest
plumage.bill: Slender, pointed -- more tweezer than nutcracker, specialized for extracting conifer seeds from cone scales
plumage.variability: Highly variable plumage -- some individuals show prominent yellow wing bars, others show almost none. The variability itself is diagnostic.
plumage.field_id: Small, heavily streaked finch with sharp pointed bill + subtle yellow wing flash in flight + buzzy zhreee call. Often confused with female House Finch or sparrows until the zhreee identifies it.

frequency_range.zhreee_hz: 2000-8000 (ascending sweep -- fundamental plus harmonics sweeping upward simultaneously)
frequency_range.warble_hz: 2000-10000 (complex -- multiple phrase types with variable frequency contours)
frequency_range.register: Wide range (2-10 kHz) -- the ascending sweep traverses a broader frequency range than any previous species in a single vocalization

zhreee_metrics.harmonic_content: Rich -- multiple harmonic bands sweeping upward simultaneously (sawtooth-like spectrum)
zhreee_metrics.perceived_quality: Buzzy, electrically charged, ascending zap -- distinctly 'electronic' sound quality
zhreee_metrics.waveform_analog: Ascending sawtooth wave with LFO sweep on pitch parameter
zhreee_metrics.key_property: The first distinctly 'electronic' sound in the engine -- buzzy timbre + frequency sweep = synthesizer in biological form

warble_metrics.structure: Extended buzzy warble incorporating zhreee as recurring motif within stream of buzzy trills, chattering runs, and descending warbles
warble_metrics.complexity: High -- multiple phrase types interleaved, all sharing buzzy timbre

irruptive_behavior.type: IRRUPTIVE -- stochastic presence driven by conifer seed crop failure
irruptive_behavior.trigger: Mast failure in boreal/montane conifer forests (spruce, hemlock, pine, fir)
irruptive_behavior.mechanism: Seed crop collapse in breeding range drives massive nomadic southward/downslope dispersal
irruptive_behavior.temporal_pattern: NOT periodic (no fixed schedule) -- event-driven, determined by seed crop conditions
irruptive_behavior.signal_model: Stochastic signal -- neither DC (resident) nor periodic (migrant) but event-driven colored noise
irruptive_behavior.key_distinction: First species in the engine with chaotic/unpredictable presence. Residents are constants. Migrants are periodic. Siskins are stochastic.
irruptive_behavior.information_value: Siskin presence at lowland/southern locations is a SIGNAL that seed crops have failed in boreal/montane forests -- the bird carries information about distant ecosystems
irruptive_behavior.key_study: Koenig & Knops (2001) -- irruptive movements correlated with cone crop failure across continental scale

conifer_dependency.primary_food: Conifer seeds -- spruce, hemlock, pine, fir, larch, cedar
conifer_dependency.bill_adaptation: Slender, pointed bill for inserting between cone scales and extracting small winged seeds
conifer_dependency.masting_connection: Conifer masting (synchronized boom-and-bust seed production) drives the irruptive cycle
pnw_conifers: Douglas fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) -- iconic PNW tree
Lodgepole pine (Pinus contorta) -- subalpine and disturbed

theory_nodes.concept: Ascending sawtooth wave (buzzy frequency sweep -- the electronic sound in biological form)
examples: Sawtooth oscillator with LFO on pitch: all harmonics present, amplitude decreasing as 1/n, frequency sweeping upward
Radar chirp: linear frequency sweep used for range detection -- the siskin's zhreee is a biological chirp
Synthesizer filter sweep: the classic 'wah' effect of sweeping a filter across a harmonically rich oscillator
The siskin's zhreee: multiple harmonic bands sweeping upward simultaneously on the spectrogram
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The zhreee is a sawtooth wave with an ascending frequency sweep. In synthesizer terms: engage a sawtooth oscillator, apply an LFO to pitch with positive modulation, sweep low to high over 0.3-0.8 seconds. The result is a buzzy, harmonically rich, ascending sound. The siskin IS a synthesizer.

theory_nodes.concept: Irruptive rhythm as free jazz timing (stochastic presence -- event-driven, not clock-driven)
examples: Free jazz: rhythmic events occur when conditions demand, not on a metered grid
John Cage's Music of Changes (1951): compositional decisions determined by I Ching (external conditions)
Xenakis stochastic music: probability distributions governing individual events, deterministic statistical properties
Pine Siskin irruption: presence determined by seed crop failure (external condition), not by calendar (internal clock)
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The siskin's irruptive presence is the ecological equivalent of free jazz timing. In traditional music, events occur on a grid (the beat) or in predictable relationship to it. In free jazz, events occur when conditions demand. The siskin arrives not on schedule but on crisis. Clock-driven becomes event-driven.

theory_nodes.concept: Buzzy timbre as harmonic density (sawtooth/pulse waveform with all harmonics present)
examples: Sawtooth wave: all harmonics (odd + even), amplitude 1/n -- the buzziest standard waveform
Square/pulse wave: odd harmonics only -- buzzy but less dense than sawtooth
Sine wave: no harmonics -- pure, smooth, not buzzy at all
Siskin zhreee: harmonically dense, rough-textured, electrically charged -- sawtooth-like spectrum
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: 'Buzzy' = dense harmonic content. A sine wave is smooth. A sawtooth is buzzy. The siskin's vocalizations are consistently buzzy -- every call shares this rough, harmonically rich, electrically charged timbre. The biological voice converges with the electronic instrument.

theory_nodes.concept: Three temporal modes (DC / periodic / stochastic -- resident / migrant / irruptive)
examples: Resident (DC signal): present year-round, constant -- Great Blue Heron, Pacific Wren, chickadees, junco
Migrant (periodic signal): arrives and departs on calendar schedule, 12-month period -- Pacific-slope Flycatcher
Irruptive (stochastic signal): present or absent based on external resource conditions, no fixed schedule -- Pine Siskin
The three modes exhaust the major categories of animal presence: constant, periodic, event-driven
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Resident = whole note held indefinitely (DC). Migrant = oscillation with fixed period (sine wave). Irruptive = event-driven trigger, no fixed timing (stochastic process). The siskin completes the taxonomy of temporal modes in the engine.

theory_nodes.concept: Conifer masting as boom-and-bust resource cycle
examples: Masting: synchronized seed production across large areas -- evolutionary predator-satiation strategy
Mast years: abundant food, high breeding success, population increase in seed-dependent species
Mast failure years: food scarcity, breeding failure, irruptive dispersal of seed-dependent species
The siskin's irruptive cycle is the dispersal phase of the masting boom-and-bust
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Masting is a biological crescendo-decrescendo cycle at multi-year timescales. The boom years are fortissimo -- abundant resources, high productivity, full orchestra. The bust years are the silence between movements. The siskin's irruption is the fermata -- the pause before the next movement, filled with restless motion.

theory_nodes.concept: Nomadic movement as stochastic composition (Xenakis probability distributions in ecology)
examples: Xenakis Metastaseis (1954): glissandi determined by mathematical functions, individual paths unpredictable, aggregate behavior deterministic
Xenakis Pithoprakta (1956): note events governed by Maxwell-Boltzmann gas dynamics distributions
Pine Siskin irruption: individual bird movements unpredictable, aggregate population follows resource gradient
Individual chaos, collective pattern -- the defining characteristic of both stochastic music and irruptive migration
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Xenakis used probability distributions to govern individual note events while making the statistical properties of the whole deterministic. Each siskin's flight path is unpredictable. The aggregate movement follows the seed crop gradient. Micro-stochastic, macro-deterministic. Pithoprakta in feathers.

cross_references.title: Pacific-slope Flycatcher (Degree 6)
cross_references.connection: FIRST MIGRANT VS. FIRST IRRUPTIVE. The flycatcher was the first non-resident species -- a seasonal migrant with predictable, calendar-driven arrivals and departures (periodic signal). The siskin is the first irruptive species -- present or absent based on seed crop conditions (stochastic signal). Together with resident species (DC signal), they define the three temporal modes of the engine.

cross_references.title: Built & Physical Systems
cross_references.connection: FREQUENCY SWEEP. The siskin's ascending zhreee is a linear frequency sweep -- the same signal used in radar chirps, audio test sweeps, and signal processing. BPS documents frequency-domain analysis and signal processing. The siskin provides the biological frequency sweep -- a chirp signal in feathers.

cross_references.title: LED Research
cross_references.connection: SAWTOOTH WAVE. The zhreee's harmonic structure maps to a sawtooth waveform -- all harmonics present, amplitude decreasing as 1/n. LED documents electronic systems including PWM and signal generation. The siskin's buzzy timbre is the acoustic analog of a harmonically rich electronic waveform.

cross_references.title: Shelter / Electronics Research
cross_references.connection: THE ELECTRONIC SOUND. The siskin is the first species in the engine whose vocalizations sound distinctly 'electronic' -- buzzy, synthesizer-like, harmonically dense. SHE documents electronic systems. The biological voice and the electronic instrument converge in the siskin's zhreee.

cross_references.title: Seattle 360 Music
cross_references.connection: Part A parallel: Lanphere at degree 12. Both are energy-1. Both disappear and return unpredictably. Both are defined by absence as much as presence. Lanphere's arc (present/absent/present) parallels the siskin's irruptive cycle (present/absent/present). Both carry more significance when they appear because the appearance cannot be scheduled.

cross_references.title: Bushtit (Degree 10)
cross_references.connection: ENERGY-1 CONTRAST. Both are energy-1, both are small, both are heard in flocks. But the bushtit is RESIDENT -- always present, whispering constantly (DC quiet signal). The siskin is IRRUPTIVE -- sometimes present, sometimes absent for years (stochastic quiet signal). Same energy, opposite temporal behavior.

cross_references.title: Oregon Junco (Degree 11)
cross_references.connection: ADJACENT DEGREES, CONTRASTING STRATEGIES. The junco is Emberizidae at the forest edge, resident, single-pitch trill. The siskin is Fringillidae in the conifer canopy, irruptive, ascending frequency sweep. The junco's trill is the analog-to-digital boundary (discrete-to-continuous). The siskin's zhreee is the ascending sawtooth (frequency sweep with harmonic richness). Sequential degrees exploring fundamentally different acoustic and ecological strategies.

cross_references.title: Living Systems of the PNW
cross_references.connection: CONIFER SEED CROP ECOLOGY. The siskin's entire life history is driven by conifer masting dynamics -- the boom-and-bust cycle of seed production that shapes PNW forest ecosystems. ECO documents these forest systems and their processes. The siskin is the mobile indicator of conifer forest health -- its presence signals seed crop conditions across the region.

cross_references.title: Cascade Range Research
cross_references.connection: MONTANE CONIFER HABITAT. The Cascade Range is core Pine Siskin breeding habitat in Washington -- subalpine fir, mountain hemlock, lodgepole pine, Engelmann spruce. CAS documents the Cascade conifer belt. The siskin connects lowland observation points to Cascade headwater forests through its irruptive movements.

interspecies_network.status: NOT EXTENDED -- the Pine Siskin does not participate in the chickadee alarm broadcasting network. It forms its own flocking network with conspecifics and occasionally associates with goldfinch, crossbill, and other finch flocks.
interspecies_network.siskin_role: The Pine Siskin forms dense conspecific flocks (dozens to hundreds) that move through conifer canopy and visit feeders. It does not participate in the chickadee alarm protocol but benefits from general alarm behavior in mixed-species flocks. The siskin's communication is INTRASPECIFIC: zhreee calls maintain flock cohesion during flight and foraging, functioning like the bushtit's contact seep but with a richer, more distinctive signal. The siskin introduces a FOURTH communication modality to the forest: the buzzy frequency-sweep flock call.
interspecies_network.note: The alarm network remains at 4 nodes. The swarm cohesion network (bushtit) remains at 1 node. The trill territorial network (junco) remains at 1 node. The siskin adds a fourth modality: buzzy frequency-sweep intraspecific flock communication. Four parallel communication channels now documented in the forest.

part_a_parallel.shared_principle: Both are energy-1. Both disappear and return unpredictably. Both are defined by absence as much as presence. The irruptive cycle and Lanphere's arc share the same temporal structure: present, then absent, then present again -- with the return carrying weight precisely because it could not be scheduled.
resonance_points: Unpredictable presence: Lanphere's appearances in the Seattle music scene were not on a fixed schedule -- periods of visibility alternated with periods of absence. The siskin's presence at any given location is similarly stochastic -- driven by external conditions, not a calendar.

Defined by absence: Both carry more significance when they appear because the appearance cannot be taken for granted. A resident species (or a musician who is always at the same club every Tuesday) becomes background. An irruptive species (or a musician who surfaces unpredictably) commands attention when present.

Energy 1: Both are quiet. The siskin's zhreee is distinctive but not loud. Lanphere's energy level is similarly restrained -- not a powerhouse projection but a distinctive, recognizable signal at close range.

Redemption arc structure: Present, absent, present. The return is not guaranteed. When it happens, it resets the context. The siskin's irruptive return to a location after years of absence is an ecological redemption arc -- the forest's seed crop has recovered, the bird is back, the cycle continues.

degree_arc.position: The rising zhreee -- ascending buzzy sweep, the first electronic sound in biological form. The first irruptive species. Stochastic presence.
degree_arc.progression: Silence (0) -> cascade complexity (1) -> sustained tone (2) -> referential syntax (3) -> rhythmic motif (4) -> steady beat (5) -> upslurred question (6) -> harmonic series melody (7) -> ultrasonic threshold (8) -> variation on a theme (9) -> minimum viable signal / swarm (10) -> metallic trill / discrete-to-continuous (11) -> ascending buzzy sweep / irruptive stochastic (12)
degree_arc.key_insight: Degree 12 introduces three new dimensions: (1) THE ELECTRONIC SOUND -- the first species whose vocalizations sound distinctly like a synthesizer, with buzzy timbre mapping to sawtooth waveform and ascending pitch sweep mapping to LFO on oscillator pitch. (2) IRRUPTIVE MIGRATION -- the first species with stochastic (non-resident, non-migratory) presence, driven by conifer seed crop failure, introducing noise/randomness into the temporal model. (3) INFORMATION CARRIER -- the siskin's presence is a signal about distant ecosystem conditions; the bird carries data about forests you cannot see.

synthesis_types.degree_4: Rhythmic cell (motif -- 4 notes with internal temporal structure)
synthesis_types.degree_5: Metronomic pulse (single note, steady rate -- pure temporal reference)
synthesis_types.degree_6: Pitch sweep (continuous ascending frequency glide -- directional change)
synthesis_types.degree_7: Harmonic series navigation (pitch selection from overtone ratios -- mathematical melody)
synthesis_types.degree_8: Ultra-high ascending series (discrete ascending notes at the audiometric boundary -- threshold crossing)
synthesis_types.degree_9: Variation on combinatorial syntax (same A-B-C-D structure, modified tempo/pitch/timbre -- the cover version)
synthesis_types.degree_10: Minimum viable signal (single-type contact call at noise floor -- swarm cohesion from 1-bit presence)
synthesis_types.degree_11: Tremolo / trill (single-note rapid repetition above fusion threshold -- discrete pulses perceived as continuous tone)
synthesis_types.degree_12: Ascending sawtooth sweep (harmonically rich buzzy waveform with ascending frequency -- the electronic sound in biological form)

register_progression.degree_5: Mid-range (1.5-3.5 kHz fundamental, harmonics to 4+ kHz)
register_progression.degree_7: Mid-range to treble (1.5-4.0 kHz fundamental, overtones higher)
register_progression.degree_8: Ultra-high (6-10 kHz -- above audiometric ceiling for many adults)
register_progression.degree_9: Alto-soprano (2.5-5.5+ kHz -- shifted higher than degree 3, same register zone)
register_progression.degree_10: Ultra-high / noise floor (6-9 kHz -- in the same ultra-high register as degree 8 but quieter and structurally simpler)
register_progression.degree_11: Mid-range (3-6 kHz -- comfortably within human hearing, return to accessible mid-range)
register_progression.degree_12: Wide-band (2-10 kHz -- the ascending sweep traverses more of the frequency spectrum than any previous single vocalization)

content_progression.degree_1: Identity + fitness (who is singing, how well)
content_progression.degree_3: Referential threat information (what is there, how dangerous)
content_progression.degree_4: Territorial identity through rhythm (I am here, this is my pattern)
content_progression.degree_5: Temporal grid (the steady beat that locates all other rhythms in time)
content_progression.degree_6: Identity through voice alone (the call IS the species)
content_progression.degree_7: Mathematical consonance (pitch selection follows the harmonic series)
content_progression.degree_8: Conspecific high-canopy signal (private channel at top of frequency band)
content_progression.degree_9: Referential threat information (SAME as degree 3 -- the content is conserved; the delivery varies)
content_progression.degree_10: Pure presence / flock cohesion (I am here -- no predator encoding, no territory, no mate attraction. 1 bit.)
content_progression.degree_11: Territory + mate attraction through trill (I am here, I am fit, this is my edge)
content_progression.degree_12: Flock cohesion + identity through buzzy sweep (I am here, I am Pine Siskin, where are we going -- nomadic coordination)

ecosystem_progression.degree_9: Coastal conifer (wet maritime -- Sitka spruce, western hemlock, western redcedar)
ecosystem_progression.degree_10: Shrubby woodland edge (ecotone -- the transition zone between forest and open habitat)
ecosystem_progression.degree_11: Forest edge (ecotone -- the threshold between forest interior and open ground)
ecosystem_progression.degree_12: Conifer seed crop (not a fixed habitat but a resource -- the siskin goes where the seeds are, making its ecosystem a FUNCTION of seed availability rather than a fixed geographic location)

ecology.habitat_requirement: Conifer forests with seed-bearing trees (spruce, hemlock, pine, fir, larch, cedar). Breeding habitat is boreal and montane conifer forest. Winter/irruption habitat is wherever conifer seeds, birch catkins, alder catkins, or feeder sunflower seeds are available.
ecology.niche: Conifer seed specialist -- nomadic, irruptive, following the resource. The siskin's niche is defined by a food source rather than a geographic location.
ecology.foraging_strategy: Canopy and mid-story gleaner in conifer trees -- hangs acrobatically from cone clusters, inserting slender bill between cone scales to extract seeds. Also feeds on birch and alder catkins, weed seeds, and readily visits bird feeders (thistle/nyjer seed is a strong attractant).
ecology.diet: Seeds (conifer seeds in breeding season; thistle, nyjer, sunflower, alder catkins, birch catkins in winter/irruption), insects (especially in breeding season for nestlings -- aphids, caterpillars, small beetles), buds.
ecology.nesting: Nest typically in conifer trees, 10-50 feet up, well-concealed on a horizontal branch. Cup nest of twigs, bark strips, rootlets, lined with fine plant material, fur, feathers, and thistle down. Often nests in loose colonies when seed crops are abundant.
ecology.flock_size: Variable -- small groups (5-20) to large flocks (hundreds) during irruptions
ecology.salmonellosis_vulnerability: HIGH -- dense flock feeding at contaminated feeders causes periodic die-offs. Clean feeders are critical during irruption years.

conservation.trend: Long-term decline (~1-2% per year since 1960s per BBS data, but signal is noisy due to irruptive variation in counts)
conservation.threats: Salmonellosis at bird feeders (siskins are particularly susceptible), domestic cat predation, window strikes, conifer habitat loss from logging, climate change altering masting patterns and fire regimes, northward shift of boreal spruce belt increasing energetic costs of irruptive dispersal
conservation.vulnerability: Moderately resilient but declining. The irruptive behavior makes population monitoring difficult -- Christmas Bird Count and BBS numbers swing wildly year to year depending on irruption status.
conservation.unique_feature: The irruptive behavior itself is a conservation concern: it makes the species appear abundant in irruption years and absent in non-irruption years, potentially masking long-term decline. The dependence on conifer seed crops makes the species vulnerable to any factor that disrupts masting cycles (climate change, forest fragmentation, fire regime alteration).

key_sites.name: Seattle backyard feeders (irruption years only)
key_sites.description: In irruption years, Pine Siskins flood into Seattle-area feeders, sometimes in flocks of 50+. In non-irruption years, feeders may see zero siskins for the entire winter. The contrast is extreme.

key_sites.name: Cascade Range montane forests
key_sites.description: Subalpine fir, mountain hemlock, and Engelmann spruce forests in the Washington Cascades are core breeding habitat when seed crops are adequate.

key_sites.name: Olympic National Park conifer forests
key_sites.description: Sitka spruce and western hemlock forests support siskins when cone crops are productive. Presence varies year to year.

key_sites.name: Mount Rainier National Park subalpine zone
key_sites.description: High-elevation conifer forests with mountain hemlock and subalpine fir provide breeding habitat and seed resources.

spectrogram_reference.status: ADDRESSED -- Macaulay Library provides recordings showing the ascending frequency sweep of the zhreee with multiple harmonic bands, and the extended buzzy warble
spectrogram_reference.note: Spectrographic analysis of the Pine Siskin zhreee shows MULTIPLE PARALLEL ASCENDING LINES -- the fundamental frequency plus overtones all sweeping upward simultaneously. This is the spectral signature of a sawtooth-like waveform with a frequency sweep. Compare to the Pacific-slope Flycatcher (degree 6) whose ascending whistle shows a SINGLE ascending line (pure tone / sine wave). The siskin's multi-band ascending sweep vs. the flycatcher's single-band ascending sweep is the visual difference between a sawtooth and a sine wave on a spectrogram.
